atlas.title: The Vineyard Atlas
atlas.subtitle: A Compendium of the World's Greatest Vineyards
atlas.organising_principle: Organised by Grape Variety and Blend

introduction:
This document maps the world's most revered vineyard sites to the grape varieties that have made them famous. For each variety or blend, the most notable vineyards are listed alongside their terroir, the key producers who farm them, and a brief description of what makes each site unique. The focus is on vineyards whose names carry the weight of generations — sites where geology, microclimate, and human stewardship have converged to produce wines of transcendent quality.

The selection spans the traditional heartlands of European viticulture and the most compelling New World sites that have earned comparable stature. Within each section, vineyards are grouped by region and ordered roughly by prestige and historical significance. Where a vineyard is a monopole (farmed by a single estate), this is noted.

The atlas covers twenty-two grape varieties and blend categories across approximately 300 sites. Alongside the single-variety chapters, one cross-variety category is included — Volcanic Terroirs — because volcanic soils are unusual enough in the wine world, and sufficiently distinctive in what they contribute to a wine, that the terroir itself is worth treating as a category rather than scattering the sites across their respective grape chapters.

chapters.title: Riesling
chapters.intro: No grape variety is more transparent to its site than Riesling. The roll-call of named Riesling vineyards — from the vertiginous slate slopes of the Mosel to the Permian sandstone of Kamptal — constitutes perhaps the most precisely documented terroir hierarchy outside Burgundy. The VDP's Grosse Lage classification and Alsace's Grand Cru system provide formal frameworks, but the true measure is in the glass: Riesling from a great site tastes like no other place on earth.

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — blue Devonian slate, south-facing, 60%+ gradient
sites.signature_character: Ethereal delicacy, laser precision, floral lift over wet slate; iconic for Spätlese and Auslese

sites.region_terroir: Saar — grey/red weathered slate, wind-exposed, 190–310m elevation
sites.signature_character: Crystalline purity, steely acidity, extraordinary longevity; TBAs command auction records

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — steep slate above Bernkastel, micro-sheltered
sites.signature_character: Concentration and warmth unusual for the Mosel; historic prestige rivalling top Burgundy prices

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — red slate, tiny south-facing amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: Opulent, almost exotic richness; the Mosel's most concentrated single site

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — red volcanic slate, iron-rich
sites.signature_character: Spicy, exotic character (the name means 'spice garden'); unique among Mosel sites

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — fine Devonian slate, south-facing, up to 80% grade
sites.signature_character: Power and elegance in balance; on international wine lists for over a century

sites.region_terroir: Mosel — slate amphitheatre facing south
sites.signature_character: Rich, golden-fruited; the Mosel's most generous and approachable Grand Cru

sites.region_terroir: Ruwer — blue slate, sheltered tributary valley
sites.signature_character: Weightless intensity, extraordinary aromatic complexity; whispered rather than declared

sites.region_terroir: Nahe — volcanic porphyry/slate, south-facing steep slope
sites.signature_character: Mineral depth, aristocratic structure; arguably the Nahe's single greatest site

sites.region_terroir: Nahe — volcanic soils, steep
sites.signature_character: Monumental power and wet-stone minerality; staggering concentration in top vintages

sites.region_terroir: Nahe — grey slate, steep
sites.signature_character: Radically flinty, grapefruit-driven; a site unknown until Tim Fröhlich revealed its potential

sites.region_terroir: Rheingau — Phyllite slate, gentle south-facing slope
sites.signature_character: Aristocratic elegance; the Rheingau's finest GG and a benchmark for the region's style

sites.region_terroir: Rheingau — loess/clay over limestone, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Unusual power and density for the Rheingau; rich, ripe, built for decades

sites.region_terroir: Rheingau — steep quartzite/slate above the Rhine
sites.signature_character: Stony, muscular; the most structured and age-worthy Rheingau wines

sites.region_terroir: Rheinhessen — clay over limestone (Burgundy-like)
sites.signature_character: Textural complexity recalling white Burgundy; layered, saline finish of extraordinary length

sites.region_terroir: Rheinhessen — limestone/marl
sites.signature_character: Keller's rarest bottling now commands prices rivalling Egon Müller

sites.region_terroir: Pfalz — red sandstone/limestone, Birkweiler
sites.signature_character: Enormous power with a wet-stone finish; filigreed or muscular depending on the hand

sites.region_terroir: Pfalz — basalt/sandstone/loam, sheltered
sites.signature_character: Historically the Pfalz's most prized parcel; opulent, exotic, full-bodied

sites.region_terroir: Pfalz — basalt-scattered sandstone
sites.signature_character: Deep, powerful, with a distinctive smoky-stony undertone

sites.region_terroir: Alsace — Muschelkalk limestone, Hunawihr
sites.signature_character: Austere, bone-dry, mineral; one of the world's greatest dry whites at any price

sites.region_terroir: Alsace — volcanic tuff, extremely steep south-facing, southernmost GC
sites.signature_character: Volcanic intensity, smoky, almost Etna-like; the most extreme Grand Cru in Alsace

sites.region_terroir: Alsace — granite, steep terraces, Kaysersberg
sites.signature_character: The first classified GC (1975); pure, linear, granite-driven minerality

sites.region_terroir: Alsace — marl/gypsum, Riquewihr
sites.signature_character: Famously rich and long-lived, the most opulent of the top Alsace Grands Crus

sites.region_terroir: Kamptal — Permian desert sandstone, warm micro-site
sites.signature_character: Exotic spice, unique mineral profile from ancient sandstone; a singular Austrian terroir

sites.region_terroir: Wachau — primary rock/gneiss, terraced, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Crystalline power, enormous concentration; Knoll's crown jewel

sites.region_terroir: Wachau — primary rock terraces, steep, Dürnstein
sites.signature_character: Monumental structure and longevity; the Wachau's most celebrated dry Riesling site

sites.region_terroir: Wachau — primary rock/gneiss, warm south-facing, Weissenkirchen
sites.signature_character: Savoury, herbal edge with enormous depth; among the warmest and most powerful Wachau sites

chapters.title: Chardonnay
chapters.intro: Chardonnay's greatness is anchored in the Côte de Beaune, where the limestone slopes of Puligny-Montrachet, Chassagne-Montrachet, Meursault, and Corton have defined the grape's highest expression for centuries. The Grands Crus here are the white wine equivalent of Romanée-Conti and Chambertin. Beyond Burgundy, a handful of sites in California, Argentina, Australia, and South Africa have earned recognition for producing Chardonnay of comparable ambition and terroir transparency.

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — mid-slope limestone/marl, Puligny/Chassagne
sites.signature_character: The single greatest white wine vineyard on earth: power, complexity, and longevity in one

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — higher slope, thinner soil, more limestone
sites.signature_character: More mineral and tensile than Montrachet; pure, vertical, electrifying acidity

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — lower slope, deeper clay/marl
sites.signature_character: Richer, more generous than its upslope neighbours; opulent texture, exotic fruit

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — upper slope, limestone/marl, Aloxe-Corton
sites.signature_character: Austere power; needs a decade to unfurl its smoky, mineral, white-flower complexity

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — stony limestone, Meursault
sites.signature_character: Widely considered Meursault's finest climat; tighter and more mineral than Génevrières

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Beaune — mid-slope limestone, adjacent to Bienvenues-Bâtard
sites.signature_character: Silky elegance, citrus blossom purity; the premier cru closest to Grand Cru quality

sites.region_terroir: Chablis — Kimmeridgian marl/limestone, south-facing
sites.signature_character: The most powerful and age-worthy Chablis; steel, oyster shell, extraordinary tension

sites.region_terroir: Chablis — Kimmeridgian limestone, sheltered amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: The most elegant Chablis Grand Cru; floral, nuanced, with less muscle than Les Clos

sites.region_terroir: Sonoma Coast — extreme coastal ridge, fog-influenced
sites.signature_character: Mythic California Chardonnay; power and richness balanced by coastal tension

sites.region_terroir: Russian River Valley — Goldridge sandy loam
sites.signature_character: A source vineyard shared by elite producers; tropical richness with mineral undertow

sites.region_terroir: Sonoma — volcanic/clay soils, Carneros-influenced
sites.signature_character: Rich, layered, with a distinctive earthy savouriness from volcanic soils

sites.region_terroir: Carneros — shallow clay over volcanic bedrock
sites.signature_character: Intense, concentrated; one of Carneros' oldest and most respected plantings

sites.region_terroir: Santa Maria Valley — cool maritime, sandy/clay loam
sites.signature_character: Benchmark cool-climate California Chardonnay; bright acidity, citrus, and mineral precision

sites.region_terroir: Gualtallary, Uco Valley — calcareous alluvial, 1,450m altitude
sites.signature_character: Redefining New World Chardonnay: Burgundian precision at extreme altitude with saline minerality

sites.region_terroir: Adelaide Hills — cool, high altitude, loam over sandstone
sites.signature_character: Australia's most Burgundian Chardonnay zone; elegant, restrained, citrus-driven

sites.region_terroir: Walker Bay — clay/shale, maritime-cooled
sites.signature_character: South Africa's finest Chardonnay terroir: chalky minerality, ocean-influenced freshness

chapters.title: Pinot Noir
chapters.intro: Pinot Noir's spiritual home is the Côte de Nuits, where a 20-kilometre strip of limestone slope contains the most expensive and celebrated red wine vineyards on earth. The Grands Crus of Vosne-Romanée, Chambolle-Musigny, and Gevrey-Chambertin are the apex of the variety, but Oregon's Willamette Valley, New Zealand's Central Otago, and pockets of the Sonoma Coast and South Africa's Walker Bay have demonstrated that Pinot Noir can express site with comparable transparency beyond Burgundy.

sites.region_terroir: Vosne-Romanée — mid-slope marl/limestone, perfect east exposure
sites.signature_character: The most expensive vineyard on earth; transcendent perfume, endless complexity, near-immortal

sites.region_terroir: Vosne-Romanée — steep upper slope, more iron-rich clay
sites.signature_character: More structured and powerful than RC; dark-fruited, spiced, with extraordinary depth

sites.region_terroir: Chambolle-Musigny — thin topsoil, Bajocian limestone
sites.signature_character: Ethereal silk and iron fist; the most feminine Grand Cru with phenomenal aromatic lift

sites.region_terroir: Gevrey-Chambertin — marl/limestone, east-facing mid-slope
sites.signature_character: Majesty and structure; Napoleon's favourite wine, the most regal Burgundy

sites.region_terroir: Morey-Saint-Denis — limestone/clay, complex multi-parcel GC
sites.signature_character: Wild, mineral power; deeper and more brooding than neighbouring Clos Saint-Denis

sites.region_terroir: Gevrey-Chambertin — sheltered amphitheatre, limestone
sites.signature_character: Widely considered Grand Cru quality; the greatest 1er Cru in all Burgundy

sites.region_terroir: Chambolle-Musigny — thin soil, upper slope adjacent to Musigny
sites.signature_character: Prices and quality rival Grand Cru; silky, perfumed, achingly beautiful

sites.region_terroir: Vosne-Romanée — deep clay/marl, mid to lower slope
sites.signature_character: Opulent, generous, exotic; the most sensual of the Vosne Grands Crus

sites.region_terroir: Willamette Valley — volcanic Jory red clay over basalt
sites.signature_character: Oregon's first and most iconic Pinot Noir zone; red fruit, earth, supple structure

sites.region_terroir: Willamette Valley — volcanic/marine sedimentary, Van Duzer wind corridor
sites.signature_character: Wind-cooled, firmer acidity; Oregon's most Burgundian sub-AVA

sites.region_terroir: Willamette Valley — marine sedimentary, sheltered
sites.signature_character: Compact, finely-grained; wines of unusual silkiness and precision

sites.region_terroir: Central Otago — schist/loess, north-facing terrace
sites.signature_character: New Zealand's most famous single Pinot block; dark fruit, minerality, Central Otago intensity

sites.region_terroir: Sonoma Coast — extreme coastal ridge, fractured sandstone/shale
sites.signature_character: True-coast Pinot Noir; savoury, herbal, with bracing acidity and wild-berry character

sites.region_terroir: Santa Lucia Highlands — granite/limestone, high-altitude terraces
sites.signature_character: Intense, dark-fruited, muscular; California's most powerful Pinot Noir site

sites.region_terroir: Rheingau — slate, steep south-facing
sites.signature_character: Germany's most famous Spätburgunder site; ethereal, translucent red fruit over mineral

sites.region_terroir: Walker Bay — clay/shale, maritime-cooled valley
sites.signature_character: Africa's premier Pinot Noir terroir: bright cherry, fine tannin, ocean freshness

chapters.title: Bordeaux Red Blends
chapters.intro: Bordeaux's greatness is inseparable from its terroir hierarchy. The Left Bank's deep gravel mounds, sculpted by glacial rivers, provide ideal drainage for Cabernet Sauvignon, while the Right Bank's limestone plateaux and clay soils favour Merlot and Cabernet Franc. The 1855 Classification, the Saint-Émilion rankings, and Pomerol's unofficial hierarchy all attempt to codify what the gravel, clay, and limestone already dictate. The sites listed here represent the geological foundations beneath the most celebrated châteaux.

sites.region_terroir: Haut-Médoc — deep gravel/sand over clay/limestone
sites.signature_character: The holy trinity of Pauillac: Cabernet Sauvignon at its most noble, structured, and long-lived

sites.region_terroir: Graves — deep Quaternary gravel over limestone/clay
sites.signature_character: Warmer microclimate than Médoc; smoky, mineral, tobacco-tinged elegance

sites.region_terroir: Haut-Médoc — Günzian gravel mounds, deep drainage
sites.signature_character: Classical Bordeaux harmony; structure and finesse in perfect balance

sites.region_terroir: Haut-Médoc — fine gravel/sand, gentle slopes
sites.signature_character: The most perfumed and silky Left Bank wines; floral, violet-scented, lace-textured

sites.region_terroir: Haut-Médoc — clay-rich gravel, cooler microclimate
sites.signature_character: Firmer, more tannic; structured and earthy wines that reward long cellaring

sites.region_terroir: Saint-Émilion — limestone plateau with thin clay topsoil
sites.signature_character: Merlot/Cabernet Franc on limestone; rich yet lifted, mineral, increasingly prized

sites.region_terroir: Pomerol — iron-rich clay (crasse de fer) over gravel
sites.signature_character: Tiny estates on unique blue clay; voluptuous, exotic Merlot of extraordinary concentration

sites.region_terroir: Saint-Émilion — limestone slopes, south/southeast exposure
sites.signature_character: Slope-grown Cabernet Franc shines here; tension, minerality, and ethereal perfume

chapters.title: Nebbiolo
chapters.intro: Nebbiolo is perhaps the most site-specific of all red grapes. Its greatest expressions come from the Langhe hills of Piemonte, where the Barolo and Barbaresco zones are divided into named crus (Menzioni Geografiche Aggiuntive) whose terroir differences are as pronounced as those between Burgundy's Grands Crus. The distinction between Tortonian (younger, sandier) and Helvetian (older, more compact) marls in Barolo creates two fundamentally different styles of wine, and knowing which commune and cru a Barolo comes from is essential to understanding the wine in the glass.

sites.region_terroir: Barolo — south-facing, mixed marl/sand/clay, central Barolo commune
sites.signature_character: The most historically celebrated Barolo cru; harmonious, perfumed, approachable yet complex

sites.region_terroir: Serralunga d'Alba — compact Helvetian marl, east-facing
sites.signature_character: Perhaps Italy's single greatest wine; massive structure, decades of ageing, iron-fisted power

sites.region_terroir: Roddi — Tortonian marl, high elevation, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Elegant expression from a lesser-known commune; fragrant, fine-grained, increasingly celebrated

sites.region_terroir: Castiglione Falletto — Tortonian marl, south-facing amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: Structured and complex; balances Serralunga power with Castiglione finesse

sites.region_terroir: Serralunga d'Alba — compact marl, steep south-facing
sites.signature_character: Modern yet deeply rooted; concentrated, mineral, with extraordinary depth

sites.region_terroir: La Morra — calcareous marl, south-southwest exposure
sites.signature_character: Elegant La Morra at its finest; floral, silky, with early approachability masking structure

sites.region_terroir: La Morra/Barolo — south-facing, transitional soils (sand/clay/marl)
sites.signature_character: Straddles two communes; combines La Morra perfume with Barolo structure

sites.region_terroir: Serralunga d'Alba — iron-rich Lequio marl, steep southwest-facing
sites.signature_character: The most powerful Serralunga site; tannic, brooding, needs decades to reveal its depths

sites.region_terroir: Barbaresco — calcareous marl, east-facing, high elevation
sites.signature_character: Barbaresco's most age-worthy cru; austere, profound, almost Barolo-like intensity

sites.region_terroir: Barbaresco — calcareous/sandy marl, south-facing amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: The quintessential Barbaresco; elegant, perfumed, with exquisite balance

sites.region_terroir: Barbaresco — calcareous marl, southwest-facing
sites.signature_character: Power and concentration unusual for Barbaresco; deep, structured, rich

sites.region_terroir: Gattinara — volcanic porphyry/granite, Alto Piemonte
sites.signature_character: Northern Piemonte's finest Nebbiolo; leaner, more austere, with alpine minerality

sites.region_terroir: Roero — marine sand over clay
sites.signature_character: Sand-driven Nebbiolo; translucent, aromatic, early-drinking but with real depth

chapters.title: Sangiovese
chapters.intro: Sangiovese is Tuscany's soul, and its greatest sites are divided between two distinct worlds: the austere, long-lived Brunello di Montalcino and the more diverse, increasingly ambitious Chianti Classico. The Gran Selezione category in Chianti Classico has formalised the idea of single-vineyard Sangiovese, while Montalcino's producers have long argued about the superiority of northern versus southern exposures. The Super Tuscan movement, which blended Sangiovese with international varieties or bottled 100% varietal wines outside DOCG rules, has produced some of Italy's most celebrated wines.

sites.region_terroir: Montalcino north — galestro/alberese, 350m, north-facing
sites.signature_character: Montalcino's most celebrated single vineyard; austere, mineral, built for decades

sites.region_terroir: Montalcino south — clay/galestro, warmer, lower altitude
sites.signature_character: Richer, more generous Brunello; ripe fruit, Mediterranean warmth, earlier accessibility

sites.region_terroir: Gaiole in Chianti — galestro/limestone, 400m, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Ethereal elegance; Chianti Classico at its most refined and age-worthy

sites.region_terroir: Panzano in Chianti — galestro/alberese, Conca d'Oro amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: Dense, structured Sangiovese from the 'golden bowl'; one of the greatest cru bottlings

sites.region_terroir: Panzano in Chianti — galestro, 350–400m elevation
sites.signature_character: Powerful yet balanced; benchmark Gran Selezione from an outstanding producer

sites.region_terroir: Castellina in Chianti — limestone/clay, 350m
sites.signature_character: Mineral, savoury Chianti Classico with real structure and ageing potential

sites.region_terroir: Chianti Classico — alberese/galestro, 350–400m
sites.signature_character: The wine that launched the Super Tuscan revolution: Sangiovese with Cabernet in a Tuscan frame

sites.region_terroir: Panzano in Chianti — galestro/alberese
sites.signature_character: 100% Sangiovese masterwork; dark, concentrated, among Italy's finest expressions of the grape

sites.region_terroir: Montalcino northeast — galestro/marl, 500m, north-facing
sites.signature_character: The birthplace of Brunello; austere, aristocratic wines that age for half a century

chapters.title: Malbec
chapters.intro: Malbec's transformation from a blending component in Cahors and Bordeaux to a world-class varietal wine is one of the great stories of modern viticulture. Argentina's Mendoza province, and particularly the high-altitude Uco Valley, has revealed that Malbec is capable of terroir expression as nuanced as any European grape — provided it is grown at elevation, on the right soils, with the right ambition. The emerging sub-regional hierarchy of the Uco Valley (Gualtallary, Altamira, Los Chacayes) is one of the most exciting developments in contemporary wine.

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley — calcareous alluvial, 1,400–1,500m altitude
sites.signature_character: Extreme altitude delivers tension, mineral precision, and floral lift rare in Malbec

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley — alluvial/limestone, 1,050–1,100m
sites.signature_character: River-stone soils; structured, savoury Malbec with fine tannins and real ageing potential

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley — gravelly alluvial fan, 1,100–1,300m
sites.signature_character: Cooler microclimate; elegant, restrained, with a distinctly mineral, almost Bordelais character

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley — sandy/gravelly alluvial, 950–1,100m
sites.signature_character: A transitional zone; richer than Gualtallary but with good freshness and structure

sites.region_terroir: Luján de Cuyo — alluvial gravel/sand, 950m
sites.signature_character: Traditional Mendoza Malbec heartland; generous, dark-fruited, plush, approachable

sites.region_terroir: Luján de Cuyo — old alluvial terraces, 980m, some of Mendoza's oldest vines
sites.signature_character: Historic first-planting sites; concentrated old-vine Malbec with depth and complexity

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley — alluvial/sand, 950–1,100m
sites.signature_character: Warm days, cool nights; balances concentration and freshness with a distinctive spice character

sites.region_terroir: Cahors, France — limestone plateau (causse), iron-rich clay
sites.signature_character: Malbec's ancestral home; darker, more tannic, with earthy iron-and-truffle complexity

chapters.title: Syrah / Shiraz
chapters.intro: Syrah's duality — cool-climate elegance versus warm-climate power — makes it one of the most versatile and exciting grapes in the world. Its ancestral home is the Northern Rhône, where the granite and schist hillsides of Côte-Rôtie, Hermitage, and Cornas produce wines of profound complexity. Australia's Barossa Valley old vines and South Africa's Swartland represent the grape's other great poles, while Washington State's volcanic terroirs have emerged as a compelling New World frontier.

sites.vineyard_site: La Turque / La Mouline / La Landonne
sites.region_terroir: Côte-Rôtie — schist and granite/gneiss, vertiginous slopes
sites.signature_character: The most famous trio in Rhône wine: La Mouline = silk, La Landonne = power, La Turque = both

sites.region_terroir: Côte-Rôtie — dark schist/iron (Brune) vs lighter gneiss/mica (Blonde)
sites.signature_character: The original hillsides; Brune gives structure and darkness, Blonde gives perfume and lift

sites.region_terroir: Hermitage — granite (Bessards), loess (Méal), granite terrace (Ermite)
sites.signature_character: Hermitage's holy parcels; Bessards = tannic granite power, Méal = generous warmth, Ermite = ethereal

sites.region_terroir: Cornas — decomposed granite, south-facing amphitheatre
sites.signature_character: The most raw and primal Northern Rhône; dark, dense, untamed, thrilling with age

sites.region_terroir: Châteauneuf — galets roulés (large, rounded stones) over red clay
sites.signature_character: Iconic heat-retaining stones; grenache-dominant but Syrah adds spine and dark fruit

sites.region_terroir: Walla Walla Valley — fractured basalt cobblestones, wind-scoured
sites.signature_character: Volcanic cobblestones produce intensely mineral, savoury, powerful cool-climate Syrah

sites.region_terroir: Barossa — deep sandy/clay loam over ironstone, warm continental
sites.signature_character: Pre-phylloxera vines up to 170 years old; opulent, spicy, concentrated Shiraz of immense depth

sites.region_terroir: Eden Valley — sandy loam over schist, 400–500m elevation
sites.signature_character: Cooler, more peppery and restrained than Barossa floor; elegant Shiraz with real tension

sites.region_terroir: McLaren Vale — diverse: sand, clay, ironstone, limestone
sites.signature_character: Maritime influence gives freshness to rich, dark Shiraz; chocolate, earth, and olive tapenade

sites.region_terroir: Swartland — decomposed granite hillsides, dryland farmed
sites.signature_character: South Africa's most exciting Syrah terroir; savoury, peppery, with granite-driven minerality

sites.region_terroir: Washington State — windblown loess/sand over basalt, arid, warm
sites.signature_character: The warmest Washington AVA; powerful, dark-fruited, with firm tannic structure

chapters.title: Cabernet Franc
chapters.intro: Cabernet Franc is Bordeaux's secret weapon and the Loire Valley's noblest red grape. In the tuffeau limestone caves and slopes of Chinon, Bourgueil, and Saumur-Champigny, it produces wines of extraordinary purity — lighter in colour than Cabernet Sauvignon but often more aromatically complex, with signature notes of violets, graphite, crushed herbs, and wet stone. On the Right Bank of Bordeaux, it plays a crucial role alongside Merlot at estates like Cheval Blanc and Lafleur. Increasingly, ambitious New World producers in Argentina, Friuli, and even the Finger Lakes are demonstrating that Cab Franc can express terroir with Pinot Noir-like transparency.

groups.name: Loire Valley, Bordeaux Right Bank & New World

sites.region_terroir: Saumur-Champigny — tuffeau limestone, south-facing, Loire Valley
sites.signature_character: The most legendary Loire Cab Franc estate; Le Bourg is Burgundian in complexity, silky, mineral, and immortal

sites.region_terroir: Chinon — limestone/clay slopes, south-facing, above the Vienne
sites.signature_character: One of Chinon's finest lieux-dits; structured, savoury, with herb-tinged dark fruit and chalky minerality

sites.region_terroir: Chinon — old-vine tuffeau limestone slopes
sites.signature_character: Joguet's monopole of pre-phylloxera vines; profound, age-worthy, benchmark old-vine Chinon

sites.region_terroir: Chinon — clay-limestone côteaux, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Dense and structured; one of the most tannic and long-lived Chinon terroirs

sites.region_terroir: Bourgueil — tuffeau limestone/clay over tufa caves
sites.signature_character: Bourgueil's most celebrated site; darker, firmer than Chinon, with iron and graphite notes

sites.region_terroir: Saumur — deep tuffeau limestone, cooler microclimate
sites.signature_character: Emerging as one of the Loire's most exciting terroirs; chalky purity and precision

sites.region_terroir: Saint-Émilion — limestone/clay, thin topsoil
sites.signature_character: Cab Franc reaches its Bordelais apex here; floral, spiced, violet-perfumed, with silky texture

sites.region_terroir: Pomerol — iron-rich clay over gravel
sites.signature_character: Cab Franc as co-lead with Merlot; adds aromatic lift, freshness, and mineral complexity

sites.region_terroir: Uco Valley, Argentina — calcareous alluvial, 1,400m+
sites.signature_character: High-altitude Cab Franc of startling precision; floral, herbal, with Andean mineral tension

sites.region_terroir: Friuli — Ponca (layered marl/sandstone), hilly, northeast Italy
sites.signature_character: Italy's most serious Cab Franc terroir; savoury, herbal, with distinctive ponca-driven minerality

sites.region_terroir: Finger Lakes, New York — Devonian shale/slate, lake-moderated
sites.signature_character: Emerging cool-climate Cab Franc; bright acidity, herbal lift, surprisingly age-worthy

sites.region_terroir: Washington State — loess/basalt, arid, warm
sites.signature_character: Powerful New World Cab Franc; dark fruit, firm structure, basalt-driven mineral depth

chapters.title: Rhône Red Blends (GSM)
chapters.intro: The Grenache-Syrah-Mourvèdre blend — universally known as GSM — is the Southern Rhône's defining expression, anchored by the galets roulés of Châteauneuf-du-Pape and the limestone slopes of Gigondas. Grenache provides warmth, generosity, and red-fruited sweetness; Syrah adds colour, structure, and dark-fruited complexity; Mourvèdre contributes tannin, earthiness, and ageing potential. Beyond France, the blend has found natural homes in Priorat's llicorella schist, Australia's old-vine Barossa and McLaren Vale, South Africa's Swartland, and California's Paso Robles.

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — galets roulés over red clay/sand, high plateau
sites.signature_character: The most famous single lieu-dit in Châteauneuf; deep, powerful, galets-driven warmth and concentration

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — pure sand over clay, sheltered, cool micro-site
sites.signature_character: The great contrarian; pure Grenache from sand producing ethereal, Burgundian transparency

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — galets roulés over Miocene red clay
sites.signature_character: The iconic terroir of the appellation; heat-retaining stones drive concentration and spice

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — limestone scree from Dentelles, clay/sand lower

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — gravel/clay terraces with garrigue-covered slopes
sites.signature_character: Rustic power; warmer and more earthy than Gigondas, with wild herb and dark fruit intensity

sites.region_terroir: Southern Rhône — blue clay/marl over limestone, north-facing slopes
sites.signature_character: Dense, dark, powerful; Grenache thrives on clay, producing wines of surprising structure

sites.region_terroir: Priorat, Spain — llicorella (slate/schist), steep terraced hillsides
sites.signature_character: Spain's most prestigious Grenache terroir: extreme slopes, mineral intensity, dark-fruited power

sites.region_terroir: Priorat — llicorella, some of the oldest Garnacha/Cariñena vines

sites.signature_character: Rhône varieties on granite; peppery, wild-herbed, with distinctive South African garrigue character

sites.region_terroir: Paso Robles, California — calcareous/siliceous, mountainous

chapters.title: Cabernet Sauvignon (Varietal)
chapters.intro: Beyond its role in Bordeaux blends, Cabernet Sauvignon stands alone as a varietal wine of immense power and longevity, particularly in California's Napa Valley, Chile's Alto Maipo, Australia's Coonawarra and Margaret River, and Washington State. Napa's sub-appellations — each with distinct soils and microclimates — have created a terroir hierarchy as finely graded as any in the Old World. The mountain districts (Howell Mountain, Spring Mountain, Mount Veeder) versus the valley floor benchlands (Oakville, Rutherford, Stags Leap) offer fundamentally different expressions of the same grape.

sites.region_terroir: Napa Valley — alluvial gravels, warm days, cool bay influence
sites.signature_character: California's most storied Cabernet terroir; To Kalon is the single most famous American vineyard

sites.region_terroir: Napa Valley — alluvial fan gravels, well-drained

sites.region_terroir: Napa Valley — alluvial/volcanic, sheltered palisade cliffs
sites.signature_character: Softer, more refined Napa Cabernet; iron fist in velvet glove; site of the 1976 Judgment of Paris

sites.signature_character: South America's most prestigious Cabernet terroir; Bordelais structure with Andean mineral intensity

chapters.title: Grenache / Garnacha
chapters.intro: Grenache is the Mediterranean's great grape — generous, warm-hearted, and capable of extraordinary complexity when grown on the right soils at the right yields. While it appears as a blending partner in the GSM section of this Atlas, Grenache also stands brilliantly alone: as the ethereal, sand-grown pure varietal of Château Rayas, the slate-driven Garnacha of Priorat, the ancient Cannonau of Sardinia's mountains, the granite-grown revelation of Sierra de Gredos, and the pre-phylloxera old-vine treasures of the Barossa. No grape covers a wider spectrum from rustic power to Burgundian transparency.

sites.region_terroir: Châteauneuf-du-Pape — pure sand over clay, sheltered, cool micro-site
sites.signature_character: The most Burgundian Grenache on earth; pure sand produces ethereal transparency and haunting fragrance

sites.region_terroir: Châteauneuf — galets roulés over red clay, 80–100+ year vines
sites.signature_character: Ancient vines on iconic stones; the deepest, most concentrated expression of Southern Rhône Grenache

sites.region_terroir: Gigondas — limestone scree from Dentelles de Montmirail, 300–400m
sites.signature_character: High-altitude Grenache with limestone lift; more tensile and mineral than the valley floor

sites.region_terroir: Priorat — llicorella (slate/schist), steep terraces, 200–600m
sites.signature_character: Spain's most prestigious Garnacha; extreme slopes, mineral intensity, dark power from ancient schist

sites.region_terroir: Priorat — llicorella, centenarian Garnacha/Cariñena vines
sites.signature_character: Some of Priorat's oldest plantings; concentrated, savoury, profoundly deep, low-yielding vines

sites.region_terroir: Aragon — limestone/clay, high plateau, 500–700m, continental
key_producers: Bodegas Frontonio (El Jardín de las Iguales)
sites.signature_character: High-altitude old-vine Garnacha; extraordinary value, dark-fruited, with surprising mineral precision

sites.region_terroir: Central Spain — granite sand, 800–1,200m altitude, extreme continental
key_producers: Comando G (La Bruja de Rozas, Rumbo al Norte)
sites.signature_character: Spain's most exciting rediscovery; translucent, Burgundian Garnacha from granite at extreme altitude

sites.region_terroir: Sardinia — granite/schist, mountainous interior, 500–700m

sites.region_terroir: McLaren Vale — sand/clay, maritime-moderated
sites.signature_character: Maritime-influenced old-vine Grenache; fragrant, medium-bodied, with red-fruited charm and savour

chapters.title: Tempranillo
chapters.intro: Tempranillo is Spain's noble grape, the backbone of Rioja and Ribera del Duero, and the foundation of the country's greatest red wines. In Rioja, it produces wines of elegant structure and remarkable longevity, shaped by decades of oak ageing in a tradition unlike any other wine region. In Ribera del Duero, grown as Tinto Fino at 800 metres on limestone plateaux, it becomes darker, more powerful, and more tannic. In Toro, on ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines in deep sand, it reaches its most primal and concentrated expression. The inclusion of Bierzo's Mencía — a close genetic relative — acknowledges one of Spain's most exciting terroir-driven rediscoveries.

groups.name: Rioja, Ribera del Duero, Toro & Bierzo

chapters.title: Chenin Blanc
chapters.intro: Chenin Blanc is one of the world's most versatile and age-worthy white grapes, capable of producing everything from austere, bone-dry Savennieres to lusciously sweet Quarts de Chaume, from sparkling Crémant de Loire to South Africa's finest old-vine whites. Its spiritual home is the Loire Valley, where tuffeau limestone caves and schist slopes have nurtured it for centuries. South Africa's Chenin renaissance — built on old bush vines planted decades ago for brandy production — has revealed a second great terroir for the grape, with Swartland, Stellenbosch, and the remote Citrusdal Mountain producing wines of startling quality and individuality.

sites.region_terroir: Savennieres — schist/volcanic, steep south-facing above the Loire
sites.signature_character: The most famous biodynamic vineyard in France; powerful, austere dry Chenin of extraordinary complexity

sites.region_terroir: Savennieres — schist/sandstone, south-facing slopes
sites.signature_character: Sister site to Coulée de Serrant; slightly more approachable but equally mineral and age-worthy

sites.region_terroir: Vouvray — tuffeau limestone/clay, south-facing above the Loire
key_producers: Domaine Huet (Le Haut-Lieu, Le Mont, Clos du Bourg)
sites.signature_character: The benchmark for Vouvray; Huet's three monopoles produce the full spectrum from sec to moelleux

sites.region_terroir: Vouvray — silex/tuffeau, sheltered, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Huet's most structured vineyard; concentrated, mineral, extraordinary longevity in all styles

sites.region_terroir: Montlouis — tuffeau/silex, south-facing, Loire-side
key_producers: Jacky Blot (Domaine de la Taille aux Loups)
sites.signature_character: Montlouis's finest terroir; more mineral and tensile than Vouvray, increasingly recognised

sites.region_terroir: Layon — schist/volcanic, steep south-facing amphitheatre, botrytis-prone

sites.region_terroir: Franschhoek — alluvial/granite, sheltered valley

chapters.title: Grüner Veltliner
chapters.intro: Grüner Veltliner is Austria's signature grape and its most widely planted variety, ranging from simple, peppery tavern wines to profoundly complex, age-worthy single-vineyard bottlings that rival the finest white Burgundy. The Wachau's terraced primary-rock slopes produce the most powerful and concentrated expressions, while the Kamptal's loess and primary rock terroirs deliver racy, mineral-driven wines of extraordinary precision. The Wagram's deep loess and the Weinviertel's limestone soils offer yet further terroir diversity within a single variety. At its best, Grüner Veltliner combines white pepper spice, herbal complexity, and a savoury mineral depth that is entirely its own.

sites.signature_character: Ott's flagship; the Wagram's finest GV site, with characteristic loess-driven creaminess and spice

chapters.title: Gamay
chapters.intro: Gamay's ten Beaujolais crus constitute one of the most precisely defined and geologically diverse terroir hierarchies in France. Each cru sits on a distinct combination of granite, schist, volcanic rock, or sand, producing Gamay of markedly different character — from the ethereal florality of Fleurie to the structured, age-worthy power of Moulin-à-Vent, from the volcanic mineral intensity of Côte de Brouilly to the wild, primal energy of Morgon's Côte du Py. The Cru Beaujolais renaissance, led by natural wine pioneers like Marcel Lapierre and Jean Foillard, has restored these wines to their rightful place among France's great terroir expressions. Beyond Beaujolais, Gamay d'Auvergne on volcanic soils represents an exciting frontier.

sites.region_terroir: Morgon — decomposed volcanic schist ('rotten rock'), south-facing hill

sites.vineyard_site: Moulin-à-Vent – Les Thorins / Champ de Cour

sites.region_terroir: Côte de Brouilly — blue diorite (volcanic), steep slopes of Mont Brouilly

chapters.title: Sauvignon Blanc
chapters.intro: Sauvignon Blanc's reputation suffers, unfairly, from its own ubiquity. The grape that at industrial scale gives Marlborough its pungent, passionfruit-driven house style is also the grape that, on the Kimmeridgian marl of Chavignol or the flint of Saint-Andelain, produces some of the most precise, mineral-driven, and age-worthy white wines in France. What unites the great sites is a particular kind of terroir legibility: Sauvignon Blanc, more than almost any other white grape, carries its soil directly into the glass. Limestone gives cut and citrus; flint gives smoke and tension; chalk gives weightlessness. In Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé, the Loire's upper reaches, the hierarchy of named slopes is unofficial — no Grand Cru system exists here — but is nonetheless as well-understood by growers as any classified system in France. Beyond the Loire, Bordeaux's gravel terraces in Pessac-Léognan, the calcareous marl of Austria's Südsteiermark, and the hillside plantings of Marlborough's Southern Valleys constitute the grape's other major poles of quality.

chapters.title: Alsace Grands Crus — Gewürztraminer & Pinot Gris
chapters.intro: Alsace is an anomaly: a French region whose terroir hierarchy is formally classified (51 Grands Crus since 1975), yet whose wines carry the grape name on the label in the German manner rather than the site name in the French manner. For Riesling, which appears in Chapter I, the Grand Cru system maps cleanly onto recognised quality. For the two other noble Alsace grapes — Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris — the hierarchy is less fully exploited by growers but no less real. Gewürztraminer, with its lychee-and-rose aromatic profile, can turn blowsy on the wrong site and magisterial on the right one; Hengst's calcareous conglomerate and Goldert's oolitic limestone are two of the very few terroirs in the world where the grape achieves real structure and restraint. Pinot Gris, so often forgettable in its Italian Grigio guise, here produces wines of extraordinary weight and spice — particularly on the marl-and-gypsum of Clos Jebsal or the volcanic tuff of Rangen. Several of the sites listed below appear in the Riesling chapter as well; the Alsace Grands Crus reward all three noble grapes, though rarely equally.

sites.region_terroir: Thann, Alsace — volcanic tuff, steepest Alsace GC, south-facing, extreme slope
sites.signature_character: The most singular terroir in Alsace; volcanic intensity gives Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris smoky, mineral, almost Etna-like depth

sites.region_terroir: Andlau, Alsace — blue Steige schist, steepest slope in the Bas-Rhin
sites.signature_character: Schist gives a flinty, austere edge to Pinot Gris; tight, mineral, ageable

sites.region_terroir: Wintzenheim, Alsace — calcareous conglomerate/marl, south-southeast facing
sites.signature_character: Arguably the greatest Gewürztraminer terroir in Alsace; structured, powerful, with extraordinary longevity and savoury depth

sites.region_terroir: Gueberschwihr, Alsace — oolitic Jurassic limestone, south-facing
sites.signature_character: The classic Gewürztraminer site; the name ("golden gate") describes exactly the colour and aromatic generosity the terroir delivers

sites.region_terroir: Kientzheim / Sigolsheim, Alsace — limestone/marl over schist, steep south-facing
sites.signature_character: Warm, concentrated site; produces opulent Gewürztraminer and powerful late-harvest Pinot Gris

sites.region_terroir: Riquewihr, Alsace — heavy marl/clay, south-facing
sites.signature_character: The most opulent Alsace Grand Cru for Gewürztraminer; rich, spicy, late-ripening, ideal for Vendanges Tardives

sites.region_terroir: Guebwiller, Alsace — red sandstone, warm microclimate, south-facing
sites.signature_character: Sandstone retains heat; Gewürztraminer here is rich and perfumed with distinctive mineral backbone

sites.region_terroir: Turckheim, Alsace — grey marl and gypsum, south-facing, Zind-Humbrecht monopole
sites.signature_character: The reference Pinot Gris terroir; marl-and-gypsum produces wines of enormous weight, spice, and botrytis affinity

sites.region_terroir: Wintzenheim, Alsace — iron-rich red clay/marl over limestone
sites.signature_character: A Pinot Gris specialist site; iron in the soil gives the wines a distinctive smoky, savoury note

chapters.footnote: Rangen de Thann and Kastelberg appear in the Riesling chapter; the Clos Saint-Urbain monopole is called out separately here because its Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris are as celebrated as its Riesling. Several Alsace Grands Crus not listed here (Schlossberg, Brand, Kitterlé) are primarily Riesling sites.

chapters.title: Champagne Grands Crus
chapters.intro: Champagne's 1985 échelle des crus classification rates each of the region's 319 villages on a 100-point scale; 17 villages score 100 and are designated Grand Cru. The classification is villages-not-vineyards, a single appellation-wide generalisation rather than a parcel-by-parcel hierarchy of the Burgundian kind — but within the Grand Cru villages themselves, a parcel-level terroir culture has flourished, particularly since the rise of grower-Champagne in the 1990s. The chalk subsoil, present throughout the region, takes different forms across the three main sub-districts: the deep Craie de Belemnites under the Côte des Blancs produces the region's finest Chardonnay; the colder, often shallower chalk of the Montagne de Reims favours Pinot Noir; and the Vallée de la Marne's clay-and-chalk mix is the historic home of Pinot Meunier. The named single-vineyard clos — Krug's Clos du Mesnil, Philipponnat's Clos des Goisses — sit atop this hierarchy, producing Champagnes that rival white Burgundy's Grands Crus for structure and longevity.

groups.name: Côte des Blancs — Chardonnay Grand Cru Villages

sites.region_terroir: Côte des Blancs — pure Craie de Belemnites chalk, east-facing slope
sites.signature_character: The Montrachet of Champagne; bone-dry, saline, laser-precise Chardonnay capable of decades in cellar

sites.region_terroir: Côte des Blancs — deep chalk, east-facing gentle slope
sites.signature_character: The broadest, creamiest Côte des Blancs village; generous stone fruit over chalky foundation

sites.region_terroir: Côte des Blancs — chalk, east-facing, slightly warmer than Cramant
sites.signature_character: The engine room of grower Champagne; Selosse's base of operations and the site with the most intense chalky drive

sites.region_terroir: Côte des Blancs — chalk, east-facing, between Avize and Le Mesnil
sites.signature_character: Often overshadowed by neighbours but produces Chardonnay of remarkable elegance and floral complexity

sites.region_terroir: Côte des Blancs (north) — chalk, gentler slopes, warmer
sites.signature_character: Northern Côte des Blancs warmth; rounder, more immediately charming Chardonnay

groups.name: Montagne de Reims — Pinot Noir Grand Cru Villages

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims / Grande Vallée — chalk with clay, steep south-facing
sites.signature_character: Champagne's most storied Pinot Noir village; structured, powerful, with ungrafted vines at Bollinger that produce one of the region's rarest wines

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims (south face) — chalk with clay, south-facing
sites.signature_character: The most muscular Pinot Noir Champagne village; dense, spicy, fruit-driven

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims (south face) — chalk, south-southeast-facing
sites.signature_character: Neighbour of Bouzy with marginally more finesse; home to Krug's rarest single-vineyard cuvée

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims (north face) — chalk, cold north-facing slope
sites.signature_character: The cold, chalky north face; tight, acidic, mineral Pinot Noir; a foundational component of Cristal and many top grande marque tête de cuvées

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims (north face) — chalk, cold north-facing
sites.signature_character: Verzenay's quieter neighbour; structured, slow-developing Pinot Noir

sites.region_terroir: Montagne de Reims (north face) — chalk, north-facing
sites.signature_character: Home to one of Champagne's most respected cooperatives; structured, precise Pinot Noir

sites.region_terroir: Le Mesnil-sur-Oger — walled 1.87ha Chardonnay clos, chalk, Krug monopole
sites.signature_character: The Romanée-Conti of Champagne; a single walled parcel producing one of the most expensive and compelling wines in the world

sites.region_terroir: Ambonnay — walled 0.68ha Pinot Noir clos, chalk, Krug monopole
sites.signature_character: Krug's other monopole; Pinot Noir-only, impossibly rare, release prices approach those of DRC

sites.region_terroir: Mareuil-sur-Aÿ — steep 5.5ha south-facing chalk slope, Philipponnat monopole
sites.signature_character: The steepest single vineyard in Champagne; ripens earlier than any other and produces the region's most powerful, long-lived vintage cuvée

sites.region_terroir: Mareuil-sur-Aÿ — 1ha Pinot Noir clos, chalk/clay, Billecart-Salmon monopole
sites.signature_character: A late addition to the monopole tradition (first release 1998); Pinot Noir of remarkable depth and fineness

chapters.footnote: The 17 Grand Cru villages are all represented in the Champagne appellation's 100-point échelle, but only the most wine-critical are listed here. Villages like Beaumont-sur-Vesle, Louvois, Puisieulx, Sillery, Tours-sur-Marne (left bank), and Chouilly produce Grand Cru wine but rarely drive individual-vineyard attention.

chapters.title: Volcanic Terroirs
chapters.intro: Volcanic soils are unusual enough in the wine world that they deserve cross-variety consideration. Etna's Nerello Mascalese, Santorini's Assyrtiko, Tenerife's Listán Negro, and Pico's Verdelho are all grape-specific — but the common factor is the soil, not the grape. Volcanic terroirs tend to produce wines of distinctive character: high acidity, smoky or ashy aromatics, fine-grained tannins in reds, and a kind of saline-mineral drive that is hard to replicate elsewhere. The better volcanic sites also tend to preserve old vines; phylloxera struggles in sandy volcanic substrates, and many of the world's remaining pre-phylloxera ungrafted plantings survive on volcanic islands. Etna's contrada system — a Burgundy-style parcellation of named sites on the mountain's slopes — has been the most important recent development, formalising a terroir hierarchy that growers had long recognised informally.

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Passopisciaro) — black volcanic ash and lapilli, 800–1,000m elevation
sites.signature_character: The highest commercial Etna contrada; cold nights, crystalline acidity, and an ethereal perfume recalling Pinot Noir

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Randazzo) — ancient lava flows, iron-rich volcanic sand, 700m
sites.signature_character: Richer, more textural than Guardiola; warm southern slopes of northern Etna, producing structured Nerello Mascalese

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Passopisciaro) — mixed volcanic sand and ash, 650–750m
sites.signature_character: A broad contrada with varied soils; the mid-slope engine room of Etna Nord

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Passopisciaro) — young lava and ash, 900m+
sites.signature_character: One of the highest named sites; laser-like, crystalline, tensile Nerello

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Solicchiata) — older lava flows, dense volcanic clay, 750m
sites.signature_character: Dense, mineral, age-worthy; the deepest soils on Etna's northern slope

sites.region_terroir: Etna Nord (Solicchiata) — black volcanic sand, pre-phylloxera vines, 900m+
sites.signature_character: Among the last surviving pre-phylloxera Nerello vineyards; the link to Etna's pre-modern past

sites.region_terroir: Santorini, Greece — pumice/volcanic ash (aspa), ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines, basket-trained
sites.signature_character: The most extreme white-wine terroir in the Mediterranean; wines of saline, smoky, high-acid intensity with a signature cave-aged minerality

groups.name: Canary Islands — Listán Negro, Listán Blanco & Malvasía

sites.region_terroir: Tenerife, Canary Islands — volcanic ash/pumice, ungrafted vines on braided cordón trenzado
sites.signature_character: Wild, rustic, yet ethereal reds and whites; the braided vine training is unique in the wine world

sites.region_terroir: Tenerife (northwest) — young volcanic soils, coastal-influenced
sites.signature_character: Cooler, wind-moderated slopes producing Listán Negro of striking freshness and herbal complexity

sites.region_terroir: Lanzarote — black volcanic ash pits (zocos), single vines in volcanic hollows
sites.signature_character: Among the most visually extraordinary vineyards in the world; dry-farmed Malvasía on pure volcanic ash

sites.region_terroir: Pico Island, Azores — black basalt currais walls, UNESCO World Heritage
sites.signature_character: A landscape of low stone walls sheltering vines from Atlantic winds; producing saline, smoky Verdelho unlike any other

sites.region_terroir: Auvergne, France — basalt, pumice, volcanic conglomerate, 400–500m
sites.signature_character: France's great volcanic frontier; Gamay d'Auvergne on lava with raw, smoky, mineral intensity

chapters.footnote: Several volcanic sites appear in other chapters: Rangen de Thann (Alsace, volcanic tuff) is in the Riesling and Alsace Grands Crus chapters; Soave Classico's volcanic slopes and Côte-Rôtie's mica schist have volcanic origins but are treated regionally. The sites here are included because volcanic origin is their primary terroir identity.

chapters.title: Corvina & Veneto Reds — Valpolicella & Amarone
chapters.intro: Amarone della Valpolicella is one of the few genuinely original Italian wine styles — a passito red made from air-dried Corvina, Rondinella, and occasionally Molinara, with a concentration, alcohol, and sweetness profile that has no close parallel elsewhere. The wine's modern form dates only to the 1950s, but the appassimento drying technique is ancient, and the named hillsides of the Valpolicella Classica — the original five valleys of Fumane, Marano, Negrar, San Pietro in Cariano, and Sant'Ambrogio — constitute a terroir hierarchy formalised only in 2019 with the MGA (Menzioni Geografiche Aggiuntive) system. The geological mix of marine limestone, basalt from ancient volcanism, and alluvial clays produces wines of remarkable complexity. The best modern Amarone (Quintarelli, Dal Forno, Giuseppe Quintarelli's disciples) rivals Bordeaux's first growths for longevity and rewards the same kind of patient cellaring.

sites.region_terroir: Fumane, Valpolicella Classica — limestone hilltop monopole, 320m
sites.signature_character: A rare 100% Corvina bottling on an isolated hilltop; among the most structured and age-worthy Veneto reds

sites.region_terroir: Fumane, Valpolicella Classica — limestone/basalt, south-facing slopes
sites.signature_character: Speri's flagship Amarone cru; classical Valpolicella Classica style with unusual refinement

sites.region_terroir: Negrar, Valpolicella Classica — chalky marl, south-facing historic cru
sites.signature_character: One of the oldest recognised Valpolicella crus; traditionally the source of the longest-lived Amarone

sites.region_terroir: Negrar, Valpolicella Classica — limestone, high altitude, small-berried Corvina
sites.signature_character: Masi's single-vineyard Amarone; small berries concentrate acidity, producing unusually tensile appassimento wine

sites.region_terroir: Marano, Valpolicella Classica — volcanic basalt over limestone, 450m
sites.signature_character: Volcanic influence adds smoky, mineral drive rare in Valpolicella

sites.region_terroir: Cellore d'Illasi, Valpolicella — volcanic basalt/alluvial, high-density plantings
sites.signature_character: The most influential modern Valpolicella cru; Dal Forno's obsessive viticulture (12,000+ vines per hectare) redefined Amarone's ambitions

sites.region_terroir: Negrar (border with Illasi) — limestone/clay, 400m
sites.signature_character: The most mythologised Amarone estate; traditional methods, multi-decade ageing, legendary rarity

sites.region_terroir: Mezzane di Sotto — limestone with volcanic inclusions

chapters.footnote: Soave Classico (Garganega on volcanic basalt) is a parallel Veneto terroir tradition not covered here; it belongs with the volcanic chapter or a future white-wine expansion. Bardolino and Valpolicella's eastern lowland zones produce lighter, more modestly-ambitious wines and are not included.

chapters.title: Varietal Merlot — Beyond Pomerol & Saint-Émilion
chapters.intro: Merlot as a standalone variety — rather than as a supporting player in a Bordeaux blend — has had an uneven global career. The grape's natural generosity and early-drinking appeal made it commercially successful and then, in the 2000s, deeply unfashionable. But a small number of producers have used Merlot's exceptional compatibility with clay soils to make 100% varietal wines of real seriousness. Tuscan producers, starting with Ornellaia's Masseto in the mid-1980s, demonstrated that Merlot on the blue clay of Bolgheri could produce wines rivalling the finest Right Bank Bordeaux. Castello di Ama's l'Apparita in Chianti Classico proved the same on galestro at altitude. In California, Duckhorn's Three Palms and a handful of benchland Napa sites have made the case for New World varietal Merlot. What these sites share is clay — ideally iron-rich, structured clay, the soil that defines Pomerol — and the growing consensus that Merlot is among the most site-transparent, not least-transparent, of the Bordeaux varieties.

sites.region_terroir: Bolgheri, Tuscany — blue clay hilltop, the 'Italian Pétrus', 120m
sites.signature_character: The world's most celebrated non-Bordeaux varietal Merlot; dense, structured, profoundly long-lived

sites.region_terroir: Bolgheri, Tuscany — alluvial clay with limestone, south-facing

sites.region_terroir: Suvereto, Maremma, Tuscany — clay over schist, coastal-influenced
sites.signature_character: Among the first Tuscan varietal Merlots to earn 100-point scores; ripe, generous, dense

sites.signature_character: High-altitude, low-yielding Merlot in an unusually cool Tuscan microclimate; intense, structured, mineral

sites.region_terroir: Calistoga, Napa Valley — alluvial gravel and cobblestone fan, warm northern Napa

sites.region_terroir: Stags Leap District, Napa — volcanic/alluvial, sheltered Palisades

sites.region_terroir: Atlas Peak / Coombsville area, Napa — volcanic/alluvial

sites.region_terroir: Columbia Valley, Washington — loess over basalt, windy ridge above the Columbia River

sites.region_terroir: Stellenbosch, South Africa — decomposed granite over clay, maritime
sites.signature_character: South Africa's most convincing varietal Merlot region; structured, Bordelais-style wines

chapters.footnote: The Merlot of Pomerol (Pétrus, Le Pin, Lafleur) and Saint-Émilion is covered under Bordeaux Blends in Chapter IV; these are technically varietal or near-varietal Merlot but are conventionally treated within their Bordeaux context. The sites above are those that make Merlot the named grape on the label.

notes:
This compendium covers twenty-two grape varieties and blend categories, encompassing approximately 300 vineyard sites across more than 25 countries. Together, they represent the most widely celebrated terroir hierarchies in world wine.

Editorial criteria. Sites qualify for inclusion based on established critical consensus — the presence in published hierarchies (VDP Grosse Lage, Alsace Grand Cru, Champagne échelle des crus, Côte d'Or climats, Saint-Émilion classification), sustained high scores from major critics, and reputation among the producers and buyers who specialise in each region. The Atlas is not a complete inventory of every distinguished vineyard; it aims to cover the sites that carry the most weight in informed conversation about each grape.

Further additions under consideration. Topics being weighed for inclusion in future revisions: Mourvèdre (Bandol, Jumilla, old-vine Barossa); Viognier (Condrieu, Eden Valley, Virginia); Sémillon (Hunter Valley, Sauternes, Pessac-Léognan blanc blends); and a dedicated chapter on sweet wines of noble rot (Sauternes, Tokaji, Beerenauslese).

Vineyard sites, producer associations, and terroir descriptions are based on established consensus as of early 2026. Classifications evolve; treat this document as a living reference.
